protocol: medical - supraventricular tachycardia (including atrial fibrillation) medical - tachycardia medical - ventricular tachycardia with a pulse (protocol 2 - 8)

dialogue.utterance: Dispatch to Unit 88 for a 70-year-old female with chest pain at --Address Redacted--.
Hi, I'm Dan, an EMT with the rescue squad. What made you call 911 today?
Uh, chest pain and shortness of breath, started about three weeks ago but it's getting worse.
Can you tell me your name for me?
Janet Smith.
Thank you, Miss Smith. What city or area are we in?
[CITY].
Can you explain what happened today?
I had chest pain again. I couldn't breathe well.
That helps. You are awake and oriented to person, place, and event.
We're going to take a quick look at your airway to make sure it's clear and unobstructed.
Yes.
I don't see anything blocking your airway, and your breathing seems to be coming in easily.
I'll count your breaths now. Just breathe normally for a few seconds.
I counted 38 breaths per minute. That is quite fast.
May I listen to your lungs with a stethoscope?
Go ahead.
Your lungs are clear with equal and easy air movement on both sides.
Your oxygen saturation is 98% using room air. That's a good reading for now.
I'll check your pulse now. It will just take a moment.
Your heart rate is 89 beats per minute and feels irregular. I'll make a note of that.
We're going to take your blood pressure now. This may feel slightly tight for a moment.
Blood pressure is 137/105. That's a bit elevated.
Your skin is flushed, warm, and slightly moist. Capillary refill time is normal-between 2 and 4 seconds.
Can you tell me when the chest pain started today?
Around 4:00 this afternoon.
Was there any activity, stress, or exertion when it began?
No, I was just sitting in my car when it occurred.
If 10 is the worst pain you've ever felt, how would you rate your chest pain on a scale of 1 to 10?
10. Real bad.
Does the pain move to your arm, jaw, or back?
No. It stays directly in the middle of my chest.
Any other pain in your head, back, kidneys, or shoulders?
No, just the chest.
Any dizziness, nausea, or arm weakness with the breathing or chest pain?
No. Just the chest pain and mild breathlessness during it.
Do you have any medical conditions like heart or blood pressure issues?
I have hypertrophic cardiomyopathy.
Any allergies to medications or other substances?
I have no known allergies.
Are you currently taking any medications?
None at the moment.
How long have these chest pain episodes been happening?
About three weeks now, but they come and go.
Have you spoken to a doctor or taken any treatment for these episodes before?
No, I haven't.
I've checked your skin-flushed and warm. No signs of rash, cuts, or jaundice.
Do you know what year it is?
And can you share what the date is today?
August 26th.
Good. You are oriented to time and place.
I'm reassessing your heart rate now. It has increased to 108.
We will insert an IV line in your hand for potential medications or fluids. Consent, please?
Yes, go ahead.
IV line successfully placed in the right hand, 20 Gauge. It is currently locked with normal saline.
I'll now assist you with a vagal maneuver to help slow your heart rate.
I'm ready.
First vagal maneuver attempt-no change in heart rate or rhythm noted.
Let's try another vagal maneuver. I will assist you.
Second vagal maneuver initiated. There is a positive response-heart rate slowing and rhythm improving.
A 12-lead EKG is being completed now to evaluate the heart rhythm.
Oxygen saturation is now at 95%. It's still within acceptable limits.
Respiratory rate has now dropped to 15. That is a significant improvement.
We will check your glucose level now. I need a small finger prick. Are you okay with that?
Your glucose is at the reference point.
We will now use capnography to confirm proper breathing and etCO2 levels.
EKG readings confirm atrial fibrillation with rapid ventricular response and some aberrant conduction.
Blood pressure is currently 114/84. Still high normal but improving.
Based on your EKG mapping and current symptoms, we'll follow the protocol for tachycardia and possible atrial fibrillation.
Second round of vagal maneuvers attempted. No significant deterioration since last check.
Rechecked vitals. Pulse 108, respirations 15, BP 114/84. EKG shows sinus tachycardia now.
We'll now move you using a pivot assist and H-style harness to a stretcher. That should help maintain comfort and stability.
Okay.
Placed you in a semi-Fowler's position for safety. That should help with comfort.
I'll load the stretcher into the ambulance now as everything seems stable and prepared.
Due to the rapid ventricular rate and episodes of wide complex tachycardia, your case is being treated as emergent acuity.
Thank you.
Don't worry, we'll get you there soon. I'll confirm your arrival with the [COMPANY_NAME] now.
This is a high-acuity situation, and immediate treatment at the hospital is the next step.
[COMPANY_NAME] is on standby. They've been informed of your current condition.
Medications were not administered due to the likelihood of ventricular clot formation. This was based on the transient nature of your symptoms.
We are now en route. Your vital signs have improved but will continue to be monitored.
Patient remains stable. Capnography shows an etCO2 of 40. No signs of alarm.
You are in good hands. Our goal is to get you rested and treated at the hospital.
All patient information, EKG findings, and interventions are being compiled for hospital staff.
We will enter the trauma bay, and a trauma team will be ready.
Using the draw sheet method, we will transfer the patient smoothly and safely.
[COMPANY_NAME] staff has been updated and is prepared for our arrival.
Any further changes in vitals or condition will be addressed immediately during transport.
You are safe with our team now. We are excited to pass you off to the [COMPANY_NAME] team.
I have confirmed with [COMPANY_NAME] that they have the required equipment and staff to care for this case.
Hospital staff is aware of your condition and the interventions performed.
All reports and notes containing relevant information have been secured for handover.
Everyone on site knows your condition and what prior interventions have taken place.
No major deviations in vitals or emergency interventions during transport.
And with that, we are passing care over to the [COMPANY_NAME] team. They will continue your treatment.
I hope your recovery is a swift one, and you feel better in no time.
You're in strong hands and on the way to getting the help you need.
Good work here. Time to wrap up and rest up after a fast-paced situation.
